exercises.title: Why pre-tokenization regex comes before BPE
exercises.prompt: A production BPE tokenizer applies a pre-tokenization regex (splitting at word boundaries, handling whitespace) BEFORE running merges. Explain what problems this prevents and why GPT-style tokenizers attach whitespace to the following word.
exercises.hint: Without pre-tokenization, BPE could merge across spaces/punctuation into nonsense tokens like 'the.' or merge whole phrases. Regex restricts merges to within sensible units. Leading-space tokens ('▁the') distinguish word-start from mid-word.
exercises.solution:
**What pre-tokenization does:** before any BPE merges, a **regex splits the raw text into chunks** — words, numbers, punctuation, and whitespace runs — and **merges are only allowed *within* a chunk**, never across chunk boundaries.

**Problems it prevents:**
- **Nonsense cross-boundary merges:** without it, BPE would happily merge frequent sequences that span a space or punctuation — e.g. `'the '` + `'cat'` → `'the cat'`, or `'.'` + `'\n'` → junk tokens, or `'is.'` as a single token. The vocabulary would fill with **phrase- and punctuation-glued tokens** that generalize poorly.
- **Whitespace and punctuation chaos:** runs of spaces, newlines, and tabs need consistent handling (especially for code); the regex normalizes how they're grouped.
- **Wasted vocab:** restricting merges to within words keeps tokens **linguistically meaningful** and the vocab compact.
GPT-2's famous regex, for example, splits off contractions (`'s`, `'re`), groups letters, digits, and punctuation separately, and keeps whitespace with what follows.

**Why whitespace attaches to the *following* word:** GPT-style tokenizers represent a leading space as part of the next token (e.g. `' the'` is a different token from `'the'`, often shown as `Ġthe` or `▁the`). This lets the model **distinguish a word at the start of a line/after punctuation from the same word mid-sentence**, and makes detokenization **lossless and unambiguous** — you can reconstruct exact spacing by concatenation, with no separate space tokens to manage. It also means the very common 'space + word' pattern is a **single token**, improving compression. The cost is that `'the'` and `' the'` are distinct tokens (a minor vocab redundancy), but the lossless round-trip and word-boundary signal are worth it.

exercises.title: Byte-level fallback: encode anything
exercises.prompt: Implement byte-level encoding so the tokenizer can represent ANY input — emoji, CJK, code, binary — with zero unknown tokens. Explain the 256-byte base vocabulary, how BPE merges sit on top, and why this beats a <UNK> token.
exercises.hint: Encode text to UTF-8 bytes (0-255), each byte is a base token; BPE merges frequent byte pairs into subword tokens. Any byte sequence is representable → no UNK. Rare/foreign content just uses more tokens.
exercises.solution:
```python
def to_byte_tokens(text):
    """Every string -> a list of byte values 0..255. Never fails."""
    return list(text.encode('utf-8'))      # UTF-8 handles all of Unicode

def from_byte_tokens(byte_ids):
    return bytes(byte_ids).decode('utf-8', errors='replace')

# BPE merges then operate on top of the 256 base byte tokens:
#   base vocab    = {0..255}                      (256 tokens, covers everything)
#   learned merges= {(b'th'), (b' the'), ...}     (frequent byte sequences)
# encode: text -> bytes -> apply merges greedily -> token ids
```

**The 256-byte base vocabulary:** the tokenizer's foundation is the **256 possible byte values**. Since **any** string encodes to UTF-8 bytes, and any file is bytes, the base vocab can represent **literally any input** — every emoji (multi-byte), every CJK character, every code symbol, even invalid/binary data. There is no input it can't express.

**BPE merges on top:** you then learn BPE merges **over byte sequences** (byte-level BPE, as in GPT-2/GPT-4). Frequent byte pairs (`'t'`+`'h'` → `'th'`, then `'th'`+`'e'` → `'the'`, then `' '`+`'the'` → `' the'`) become single tokens. Common English collapses into few tokens (good compression); rare/foreign content **falls back toward individual bytes** (more tokens, but always encodable). So you get **good compression for common content and universal coverage for everything else**, in one scheme.

**Why this beats `<UNK>`:** a word-level or fixed-subword tokenizer must emit an **`<UNK>` (unknown) token** for anything outside its vocabulary — and that's **lossy and destructive**: the model literally cannot see the content, can't reproduce it on output, and an attacker/edge-case input becomes a black hole. Byte-level fallback **eliminates the entire `<UNK>` failure class**: every input round-trips exactly (`encode`→`decode` is lossless), the model can read and generate *any* character or byte, and rare inputs degrade **gracefully** (just more tokens) instead of being erased. The price — a handful of extra tokens for exotic content — is far cheaper than silently dropping information. This is why every serious 2026 tokenizer is byte-level or has byte fallback.

exercises.title: Evaluating a tokenizer: compression, fairness, and special tokens
exercises.prompt: You've trained a custom BPE tokenizer. Explain how to evaluate it (compression ratio / fertility, multilingual fairness), how special tokens (BOS/EOS/PAD/chat-role tokens) must be handled to avoid security and training bugs, and the consequences of changing a tokenizer after a model is trained.
exercises.hint: Compression = bytes or chars per token (higher = better); fertility = tokens per word, compare across languages for fairness. Special tokens must be reserved, never producible by user text, and inserted correctly. Changing the tokenizer invalidates all learned embeddings → must retrain.
exercises.solution:
**Evaluating compression:** measure **bytes (or characters) per token** — higher means the tokenizer packs more text per token (shorter sequences, cheaper, longer effective context). The inverse, **fertility** = tokens-per-word, is the standard metric; ~1.1–1.5 tokens/word for English is typical. Compare your tokenizer against a baseline (tiktoken/GPT-4) on a **held-out corpus** that matches your deployment domain (if you serve code, measure on code).

**Multilingual / domain fairness:** compute fertility **per language and per content type** (English vs Hindi vs Chinese vs code). A tokenizer that's 1.2 tokens/word on English but 4× that on Thai imposes a real **cost and latency tax** on those users and gives them **shorter effective context** and worse quality. Evaluate the **gap across languages** you care about and weight the training corpus to shrink it (proportional multilingual + code data). There's a fixed vocab budget, so improving one domain's compression usually costs another's — measure the tradeoff explicitly.

**Special tokens — handle with care (security + correctness):**
- **Reserve dedicated IDs** for `BOS`/`EOS`/`PAD` and, for chat models, **role tokens** (`<|system|>`, `<|user|>`, `<|assistant|>`, end-of-turn). These must be in the vocab but **outside the range any user text can produce**.
- **User input must never be able to *forge* a special token.** If a user can type the literal string that tokenizes to `<|assistant|>` or `<|system|>`, they can **inject fake turns / escape the chat template** — a prompt-injection / privilege-escalation bug. Production tokenizers either make special tokens **unrepresentable from raw text** (only insertable by the framework) or **escape/strip** them from user content. This is a genuine security boundary, not a cosmetic detail.
- **Loss masking depends on them:** during SFT (Lesson 6) you mask loss on everything except assistant tokens, which requires the role tokens to be inserted correctly and consistently — a mismatch silently corrupts training.
- **PAD must be masked** in attention and loss, or you train on padding.

**Consequences of changing the tokenizer after training:** the tokenizer defines the **mapping from text to the integer IDs that index the embedding matrix**. The model's learned **embeddings and output projection are tied to specific token IDs**. Change the tokenizer (different vocab, different merges, even reordered IDs) and **every embedding now points at the wrong concept** — the model is effectively scrambled and **must be retrained (or at least have its embeddings re-learned)**. This is why the tokenizer is **frozen for the life of a model**, why you can't just 'upgrade' it, and why tokenizer design is a **commitment** made before the (very expensive) pretraining run. Practically: pick/validate the tokenizer *first*, on representative multilingual+code data, with the special-token scheme locked down — because you're stuck with it.
